After being questioned by law enforcement, the victim was released and returned safely to her home. The suspect was charged with abduction, assault and menacing. On November 10th, 2022, he pleaded guilty and Department of Corrections records indicate he was sentenced to two and a half years in Ohio State prisons. He is expected to be released on supervision in the fall of 2025.

What kind of man dressed in camouflage and is in the woods at 2:00 a.m.? Well, definitely not someone hoping for a chat with the police. And when police shines a light on this guy, it's not about what he's doing, it's about why he's out there hidden in plain sight. For instance, we might think of serial killers as relics like VHS tapes or payphones, but these people didn't vanish, they evolved. The old ones left trails of bodies and mocking letters. The new ones wear smiles, they blend in, and leave nothing behind but silence. And if you think DNA and smart tech made them extinct, ask yourself why this man was alone in the woods, parked like he ran out of time. I'm semi-quoting an article I read by Derek Thompson. These people were once fueled by a society that trusted strangers and glorified drifters. Well, that world is gone, but so is the silence because now we've got cameras, DNA, databases, and a culture that thinks evil needs headlines to exist. And the truth is, our systems got better, but people, they didn't. For every killer that we stop with science, another learns how to avoid the digital footprint entirely. And sometimes the one we catch, they're the ones who wanted to be known. So in my opinion, we didn't kill off the serial killer. We just made him quieter and more patient and better at pretending to be normal. So the question isn't where the serial killers and kidnappers went, it's whether we'd even notice if they were still here, because what was hiding in that park might have been the 2025 version of something we no longer believe exists.
